The process of quantization involves converting a data type with more bits, such as 32-bit floats, to fewer bits to represent the same information.
Quantization typically involves converting data types from more bits to fewer bits, for example, from 32-bit floats to 8-bit integers.
Quantization involves converting a 32-bit Floating Point (FP32) tensor into an 8-bit Int8 tensor with a specific range.
Quantization requires a quantization constant or scale, represented by the variable 'c', in order to convert the data type.
When a large magnitude value, or outlier, occurs in the input tensor, it can lead to underutilization of quantization bins, resulting in few numbers being quantized in some bins.
To address the issue of outliers affecting quantization, a common approach is to chunk the input tensor into blocks that are independently quantized, each with their own quantization constant.

Low-rank Adapters are related to Low-rank Adapter (LoRA) as LoRA is a method that reduces memory requirements by using a small set of trainable parameters, often termed adapters, while not updating the full model parameters which remain fixed.
Low-rank Adapter (LoRA) is related to QLORA Finetuning as it augments a linear projection through additional factorized projection and it is a technique used in QLORA finetuning.
QLORA Finetuning is related to 4-bit NormalFloat (NF4) as QLORA achieves high-fidelity 4-bit finetuning via the 4-bit NormalFloat quantization technique proposed.
QLORA Finetuning is related to Double Quantization as QLORA achieves high-fidelity 4-bit finetuning via the Double Quantization technique proposed.
QLORA Finetuning is related to Paged Optimizers as QLORA introduces Paged Optimizers to prevent memory spikes during gradient checkpointing from causing out-of-memory errors.
QLORA Finetuning is related to QLORA as it discusses the components and includes a formal definition of QLORA.
Quantile Quantization is the basis on which the 4-bit NormalFloat Quantization data type is built, ensuring each quantization bin has an equal number of values assigned from the input tensor.
SRAM quantiles are fast quantile approximation algorithms used to estimate quantiles in the process of Quantile Quantization.
Quantile Quantization has large quantization errors for outliers due to the approximate nature of quantile estimation algorithms.

node_2: distribution fixed up to a quantization constant
edge: When input tensors come from a distribution fixed up to a quantization constant, they have the same quantiles, making exact quantile estimation computationally feasible.
Pretrained neural network weights usually have a zero-centered normal distribution with standard deviation σ, which can be transformed to fit into a single fixed distribution by scaling σ.
Quantiles for the data type and the neural network weights need to be normalized into the range [-1, 1]
Optimal data type for zero-mean normal distributions with arbitrary standard deviations in the range [-1, 1]
Estimate the quantiles of a theoretical N(0, 1) distribution to obtain a k-bit quantile quantization data type for normal distributions
Normalize the values of the data type into the [-1, 1] range
Quantize an input weight tensor by normalizing it into the [-1, 1] range through absolute maximum rescaling
Once the weight range and data type range match, we can quantize as usual
Rescaling the standard deviation of the weight tensor to match the standard deviation of the k-bit data type
Estimate the values of the data type as per the quantile function of the standard normal distribution N(0, 1)
Symmetric k-bit quantization does not have an exact representation of zero, which is an important property to quantize padding and other zero-valued elements with no error
Ensure a discrete zeropoint of 0 and use all 2k bits for a k-bit datatype by creating an asymmetric representation

NFk is a term used to represent the resulting data type that has an equal expected number of values in each quantization bin. It is information-theoretically optimal for zero-centered normally distributed data, hence related to k-bit NormalFloat.
Double Quantization (DQ) is introduced as the process of quantizing the quantization constants for additional memory savings, indicating that DQ is associated with Double Quantization.

Quantization constants cFP32 of the first quantization are treated as inputs to a second quantization in the Double Quantization process, establishing a relationship between quantization constants and cFP32.
The second quantization uses 8-bit Floats with a blocksize of 256, indicating that 8-bit Floats are related to the second quantization in the Double Quantization process.
The 8-bit Floats are used with a blocksize of 256 for the second quantization process.
The 8-bit Floats are quantized as no performance degradation is observed, in line with results from Dettmers and Zettlemoyer [13].
The values are centered around zero by subtracting the mean from c2 before quantization, making use of symmetric quantization constants.
Paged Optimizers use the NVIDIA unified memory 3 feature for automatic page-to-page transfers between the CPU and GPU, ensuring error-free GPU processing.
The NVIDIA unified memory 3 feature works like regular memory paging between CPU RAM and the disk for efficient memory management.
